what_it_does: Maps relative JPG file paths to canonical image://design-source/<collection>/<name> addresses, acting as an address ledger for extracted source images.
observations: Contains 160 path-to-URI mappings under the image://design-source scheme.

Entries are grouped into collections such as capital-raise, company-info, deck1-2026, landing-mockups, pitch-deck, presentation-15p, recent-pitches, vi-onepager, vt-architects, vt-gatehouse, vt-property, and vt-residential.
Most filenames use p-NN.jpg with two-digit zero padding, but some collections use p-N.jpg without leading zeros.
The largest collection is recent-pitches with 49 entries.
The file only stores path keys and URI values plus a top-level count; no dimensions, checksums, or provenance metadata.
standouts: The ledger introduces a custom image://design-source URI namespace for extracted image assets.
recent-pitches dominates the dataset with 49 of the 160 entries.
Zero-padding conventions vary across collections (p-01 vs p-1).
conventions: Consistent stripping of the .jpg extension to form the URI name segment.
Relative filesystem path used as the JSON key and the canonical URI as the value.
Top-level channel, scheme, and count fields describe the ledger itself.
concerns: Inconsistent zero-padding in page numbering may break lexical sorting or address generation assumptions.
The count field is hardcoded and could become stale if entries are added or removed.
No checksum, dimensions, or version metadata to verify the referenced image bytes.
No provenance field indicating how or when the images were extracted.
The custom URI scheme lacks documented resolution rules or escaping behavior.
notes: A 160-entry address ledger that normalizes extracted JPG paths into a custom image://design-source URI namespace organized by collection and page name.
questions: What extraction process produced these paths and guarantees the count is accurate?
Does the p-NN numbering reflect intended slide/page order?
Why do some collections use p-1 while others use p-01?
Where and how are image://design-source URIs resolved to actual bytes?
Is the design-source channel identifier stable across ingestion runs?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose appears to be an address registry for source images, and the content matches that purpose, though it omits provenance and validation metadata.
intention_signals: It seems to be trying to provide stable, collection-scoped identifiers for extracted design source images so downstream tooling can reference them without depending on filesystem paths.
novelty: Application of a custom image://design-source/<collection>/<name> URI scheme to a flat set of extracted JPGs, creating a normalized address layer.
contribution: A uniform URI mapping over extracted image files, grouped by collection and page name, that downstream systems can use as a stable reference layer independent of file system layout.
summary_for_embedding: A JSON ledger mapping 160 relative JPG paths across design-source collections to canonical image://design-source/<collection>/<name> addresses.
intention_for_embedding: To create stable, collection-scoped URIs for extracted source images so other tools can reference them independently of file system layout.
